This article **is missing information** about holding companies from the Netherlands. Please expand the article to include this information. Further details may exist on the [talk page](/wiki/Talk:Holding_company "Talk:Holding company"). *(March 2023)*

is a [company](/wiki/Company "Company") whose primary business is holding a [controlling interest](/wiki/Controlling_interest "Controlling interest") in the [securities](/wiki/Security_(finance) "Security (finance)") of other companies.[[1]](#cite_note-1) A holding company usually does not produce goods or services itself. Its purpose is to own [stock](/wiki/Share_capital "Share capital") of other companies to create a [corporate group](/wiki/Corporate_group "Corporate group").

about holding companies from the Netherlands. Please expand the article to include this information. Further details may exist on the [talk page](/wiki/Talk:Holding_company "Talk:Holding company"). *(March 2023)* |

A **holding company** is a [company](/wiki/Company "Company") whose primary business is holding a [controlling interest](/wiki/Controlling_interest "Controlling interest") in the [securities](/wiki/Security_(finance) "Security (finance)") of other companies.[[1]](#cite_note-1) A holding company usually does not produce goods or services itself. Its purpose is to own [stock](/wiki/Share_capital "Share capital") of other companies to create a [corporate group](/wiki/Corporate_group "Corporate group").

Holding companies also conduct trade and other business activities themselves. Holding companies reduce [risk](/wiki/Risk "Risk") for the [shareholders](/wiki/Shareholder "Shareholder"), and can permit the ownership and control of a number of different companies. They can be [subsidiaries](/wiki/Subsidiaries "Subsidiaries") in a [tiered structure](/wiki/Subsidiary#Tiered_subsidiaries "Subsidiary").

Holding companies are also created to hold [assets](/wiki/Assets "Assets") such as [intellectual property](/wiki/Intellectual_property "Intellectual property") or [trade secrets](/wiki/Trade_secrets "Trade secrets") that are protected from the operating company. That creates a smaller risk when it comes to [litigation](/wiki/Litigation "Litigation").

In the United States, 80% of stock, in voting and value, must be owned before [tax consolidation](/wiki/Tax_consolidation "Tax consolidation") benefits such as [tax-free](/wiki/Dividends_received_deduction "Dividends received deduction") [dividends](/wiki/Dividend "Dividend") can be claimed.[[2]](#cite_note-2) That is, if Company A owns 80% or more of the stock of Company B, Company A will not pay [taxes on dividends](/wiki/Taxes_on_dividends "Taxes on dividends") paid by Company B to its stockholders, as the payment of dividends from B to A is essentially transferring cash within a single enterprise. Any other shareholders of Company B will pay the usual taxes on dividends, as they are legitimate and ordinary dividends to these shareholders.

In some jurisdictions around the world, holding companies are called **parent companies**. *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* uses the term *parent holding company*.[[3]](#cite_note-3) Sometimes, a company intended to be a pure holding company identifies itself as such by adding "Holding" or "Holdings" to its name.[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## By country

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Holding_company&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: By country")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Wiki_letter_w.svg) | This article **is missing information** about holding companies from the Netherlands. Please expand the article to include this information. Further details may exist on the [talk page](/wiki/Talk:Holding_company "Talk:Holding company"). *(March 2023)* |

### Australia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Holding_company&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Australia")]

The parent company–subsidiary company relationship is defined by Part 1.2, Division 6, Section 46 of the [*Corporations Act 2001* (Cth)](/wiki/Corporations_Act_2001 "Corporations Act 2001"), which states:[[6]](#cite_note-6)

> A [body corporate](/wiki/Body_corporate "Body corporate") (in this section called the first body) is a subsidiary of another body corporate if, and only if:
>
> :   (a) the other body:
>
>     :   (i) controls the composition of the first body's board; or
>     :   (ii) is in a position to cast, or control the casting of, more than one-half of the maximum number of votes that might be cast at a [general meeting](/wiki/General_meeting "General meeting") of the first body; or
>     :   (iii) holds more than one-half of the issued share capital of the first body (excluding any part of that issued share capital that carries no right to participate beyond a specified amount in a distribution of either profits or capital); or
> :   (b) the first body is a subsidiary of a subsidiary of the other body.

### Canada

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Holding_company&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Canada")]

Toronto-based lawyer Michael Finley has stated, "The emerging trend that has seen international plaintiffs permitted to proceed with claims against Canadian parent companies for the allegedly wrongful activity of their foreign subsidiaries means that the corporate veil is no longer a silver bullet to the heart of a plaintiff's case."[[7]](#cite_note-7)

In Canada, holding companies are commonly used to facilitate **tax-free intercorporate dividends**, allowing dividends to flow from an operating subsidiary to a parent holding corporation without immediate corporate tax, provided certain ownership and control conditions are met under Canadian tax law.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

### Singapore

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Holding_company&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Singapore")]

The parent subsidiary company relationship is defined by Part 1, Section 5, Subsection 1 of the Companies Act, which states:[[9]](#cite_note-9)

> 5.—(1) For the purposes of this Act, a corporation shall, subject to subsection (3), be deemed to be a subsidiary of another corporation, if —
>
> :   (a) that other corporation —
>
>     :   (i) controls the composition of the board of directors of the first-mentioned corporation; or
>
>         :   [Act 36 of 2014 wef 01/07/2015]
>     :   (ii) controls more than half of the voting power of the first-mentioned corporation; or
>     :   (iii) [Deleted by Act 36 of 2014 wef 01/07/2015]
> :   (b) the first-mentioned corporation is a subsidiary of any corporation which is that other corporation's subsidiary

### United Kingdom

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Holding_company&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: United Kingdom")]

In the United Kingdom, is generally held that an organisation holding a 'controlling stake' in a company (a holding of over 51% of the stock) is in effect the de facto parent company of the firm, having overriding material influence over the held company's operations, even if no formal full takeover has been enacted. Once a full takeover or purchase is enacted, the held company is seen to have ceased to operate as an independent entity but to have become a tending subsidiary of the purchasing company, which, in turn, becomes the parent company of the subsidiary. (A holding below 50% could be sufficient to give a parent company material influence if they are the largest individual shareholder or if they are placed in control of the running of the operation by non-operational shareholders.)[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11)

#### Company law

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Holding_company&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Company law")]

In the United Kingdom, the term *holding company* is defined by the [Companies Act 2006](/wiki/Companies_Act_2006 "Companies Act 2006") at section 1159.[[12]](#cite_note-12) It defines a holding company as a company that holds a majority of the voting rights in another company, *or* is a member of another company and has the right to appoint or remove a majority of its board of directors, *or* is a member of another company and controls alone, pursuant to an agreement with other members, a majority of the voting rights in that company.

### United States

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Holding_company&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: United States")]

#### Banking

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Holding_company&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Banking")]

Further information: [Bank holding company](/wiki/Bank_holding_company "Bank holding company")

After the [2008 financial crisis](/wiki/2008_financial_crisis "2008 financial crisis"), many U.S. investment banks converted to holding companies. According to the [Federal Financial Institutions Examination Council](/wiki/Federal_Financial_Institutions_Examination_Council "Federal Financial Institutions Examination Council")'s website, [JPMorgan Chase](/wiki/JPMorgan_Chase "JPMorgan Chase"), [Bank of America](/wiki/Bank_of_America "Bank of America"), [Citigroup](/wiki/Citigroup "Citigroup"), [Wells Fargo](/wiki/Wells_Fargo "Wells Fargo"), and [Goldman Sachs](/wiki/Goldman_Sachs "Goldman Sachs") were the five largest bank holding companies in the finance sector, as of December 2013[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Holding_company&action=edit), based on total assets.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

#### Utilities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Holding_company&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Utilities")]

The [Public Utility Holding Company Act of 1935](/wiki/Public_Utility_Holding_Company_Act_of_1935 "Public Utility Holding Company Act of 1935") caused many energy companies to divest their subsidiary businesses. Between 1938 and 1958 the number of holding companies declined from 216 to 18.[[14]](#cite_note-14) [An energy law passed in 2005](/wiki/Energy_Policy_Act_of_2005 "Energy Policy Act of 2005") removed the 1935 requirements, and has led to mergers and holding company formation among power marketing and power brokering companies.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

#### Broadcasting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Holding_company&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Broadcasting")]

Further information: [Media conglomerate](/wiki/Media_conglomerate "Media conglomerate")

In American [broadcasting](/wiki/Broadcasting "Broadcasting"), many major [media conglomerates](/wiki/Media_conglomerate "Media conglomerate") have purchased smaller broadcasters outright, but have not changed the [broadcast licenses](/wiki/Broadcast_license "Broadcast license") to reflect this, resulting in stations that are (for example) still licensed to [Jacor](/wiki/Jacor "Jacor") and [Citicasters](/wiki/Citicasters "Citicasters"), effectively making them such as subsidiary companies of their owner [iHeartMedia](/wiki/IHeartMedia "IHeartMedia"). This is sometimes done on a per-[market](/wiki/Media_market "Media market") basis. For example, in [Atlanta](/wiki/Atlanta "Atlanta") both [WNNX](/wiki/WNNX_(FM) "WNNX (FM)") and later [WWWQ](/wiki/WWWQ_(FM) "WWWQ (FM)") are licensed to "WNNX LiCo, Inc." (LiCo meaning "license company"), both owned by [Susquehanna Radio](/wiki/Susquehanna_Radio "Susquehanna Radio") (which was later sold to [Cumulus Media](/wiki/Cumulus_Media "Cumulus Media")). In determining [caps](/wiki/Maximum "Maximum") to prevent excessive [concentration of media ownership](/wiki/Concentration_of_media_ownership "Concentration of media ownership"), all of these are [attributed](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/attribute "wikt:attribute") to the parent company, as are [leased stations](/wiki/Local_marketing_agreement "Local marketing agreement"), as a matter of [broadcast regulation](/wiki/Broadcast_regulation "Broadcast regulation").

#### Personal holding company

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Holding_company&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Personal holding company")]

In the United States, a personal holding company is defined in section 542 of the [Internal Revenue Code](/wiki/Internal_Revenue_Code "Internal Revenue Code"). A corporation is a personal holding company if both of the following requirements are met:[[16]](#cite_note-16)

* Gross income test: at least 60% of the corporation's adjusted ordinary gross income is from dividends, interest, rent, and royalties.
* Stock ownership test: more than 50% in value of the corporation's outstanding stock is owned by five or fewer individuals.

## Parent company

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Holding_company&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Parent company")]

A **parent company** is a [company](/wiki/Company "Company") that owns enough [voting power](/wiki/Voting_interest "Voting interest") in another firm (or *[subsidiary](/wiki/Subsidiary "Subsidiary")*)
to control management and operations by influencing or electing its [board of directors](/wiki/Board_of_directors "Board of directors").[[17]](#cite_note-:0-17) The definition of a parent company differs from [jurisdiction](/wiki/Jurisdiction "Jurisdiction") to jurisdiction, with the definition normally being defined by way of [laws dealing with companies](/wiki/Corporate_law "Corporate law") in that jurisdiction.

When an existing company establishes a new company and keeps majority shares with itself, and invites other companies to buy minority shares, it is called a parent company.[[17]](#cite_note-:0-17) A parent company could simply be a company that wholly owns another company, which is then known as a "[wholly owned subsidiary](/wiki/Wholly_owned_subsidiary "Wholly owned subsidiary")".
